segments.title: A Royal Navy answer to Japan's Number 13 class
segments.summary: The opening challenge asks for a 33-knot British capital ship able to answer Japan's Number 13 design with twelve 18-inch guns.
segments.body: The brief forces speed, heavy armor, and an enormous main battery into a 58,000-ton limit. The competing designs show why machinery weight, turret layout, secondary armament, and protection cannot all be maximized at once. Their comparison also distinguishes an optimistic fast battlecruiser solution from a more conservative battleship response.

evidence.note: The challenge specifies the displacement, speed, armament, and protection requirements.
The panel compares the completed British designs and their armor and armament tradeoffs.

segments.title: Could a dreadnought carry Aegis and VLS?
segments.summary: A modernization question tests whether an old battleship hull could accept Aegis radar, helicopters, and vertical-launch cells.
segments.body: The answer is technically yes but operationally and economically doubtful. Gun blast and shock threaten delicate electronics, modern sensors demand substantial electrical power, and old barbettes complicate VLS placement. A refit deep enough to solve those problems requires dry docks and industrial capacity that could instead build a purpose-designed warship.
segments.question: How far could a dreadnought hull be upgraded with Aegis, helicopter facilities, and VLS?
segments.answerShort: It could be done after an extreme reconstruction, but blast, power, internal arrangement, and shipyard costs make a new hull more sensible.

evidence.note: The panel discusses Aegis, VLS, blast effects, power, and the scale of reconstruction.
They compare magazine space, stability, and the relatively low structural weight of VLS cells.

segments.title: Italy's 1929 answer to the Nelson class
segments.summary: A treaty-era Italian brief combines 381 mm guns, 30 knots, and Mediterranean range within 35,000 tons.
segments.body: The designers explore how the Regia Marina might exploit an early-construction allowance while responding to the Nelson class. Their solutions highlight triple-turret preferences, the meaning of standard displacement, and the temptation to hide extra fuel weight. The completed concepts anticipate features associated with later Littorio-style fast battleships while revealing awkward secondary and torpedo arrangements.

evidence.note: The panel restates the Italian displacement, gun, speed, range, and turret requirements.
The finished designs are assessed for stability, armor, secondaries, and torpedoes.

segments.title: An 80,000-ton American Pacific battleship
segments.summary: The panel sketches a maximum American fast battleship shaped by Pacific range, heavy guns, and enlarged Panama Canal locks.
segments.body: The exercise treats the ship as an alternative to the Montana class and emphasizes the US Navy's long-range Pacific requirements. Main-battery size competes with secondary guns, armor, propulsion, and the hull weight needed to support them. The discussion also shows how canal dimensions and an 8,000-nautical-mile operating radius constrain even an exceptionally large design.

evidence.note: The panel discusses main guns, Pacific range, Montana alternatives, and dual-purpose secondaries.
They consider Panamax limits and the characteristics of an American fast battleship.

segments.title: A British rival for the Kongo contract
segments.summary: A 1913 British counter-offer competes with Vickers for Japan's Kongo-class battlecruiser contract.
segments.body: The brief holds the design near Kongo's displacement and speed while asking what another British yard might offer Japan. The panel weighs ten versus twelve 14-inch guns, belt armor, secondary batteries, torpedoes, and Pacific range. The exchange explains why Japan valued ships individually superior to likely opponents and why a seemingly small armament advantage could decide a foreign contract.

evidence.note: The Kongo contract brief establishes year, nationality, displacement, speed, and armament context.
The completed rival designs are compared for guns, armor, range, and secondaries.

segments.title: German and British 90,000-ton fast battleships
segments.summary: A head-to-head 1944 exercise compares German and British approaches to a 90,000-ton fast battleship.
segments.body: The huge displacement is quickly consumed by hull structure, propulsion, armor, and heavy guns. The resulting matchup contrasts German close-range resilience and redundancy with a British preference for long-range effectiveness and faster fire. The judges conclude that the battle could turn on which ship first damages a critical system rather than on a simple paper advantage.

evidence.note: The 1944 German-versus-British brief begins and the designers confront the weight demands of a 90,000-ton ship.
The panel compares close-range resilience, long-range fire, redundancy, and rate of fire.

segments.title: An Austro-Hungarian wing-turret challenge
segments.summary: A 1920s design challenge forces same-caliber wing and centerline turrets into an Austro-Hungarian capital ship.
segments.body: Wing turrets impose difficult firing arcs, structural weight, and cross-deck blast problems. The designers nevertheless try to create a ship suited to an alternate-history Austro-Hungarian navy facing Italian cruisers near the Strait of Otranto. The exercise makes the cost of visually dramatic but inefficient gun layouts unusually clear.

evidence.note: The brief requires wing turrets of the same caliber as the centerline battery and settles on Austro-Hungary.
The designs are judged in an Adriatic operational context.

segments.title: Alternative designs for HMS Agincourt
segments.summary: Competing Agincourt alternatives explore triple 15-inch guns and twin 18-inch guns under counterfactual British technology assumptions.
segments.body: The panel builds from unbuilt Queen Elizabeth-era concepts while allowing small-tube boilers and very heavy guns to appear early. The discussion exposes the limits of the game model but also opens a broader historical point: the Royal Navy routinely generated many paper alternatives to test costs, layouts, and political proposals. Those studies helped the Admiralty explain why attractive concepts were not necessarily practical refits or production ships.

evidence.note: The Agincourt alternatives are introduced with triple 15-inch and twin 18-inch preferences.
The panel explains the Royal Navy's extensive use of paper designs and refit studies.

segments.title: What should a Type 83 destroyer class be called?
segments.summary: A naming question links a future Type 83 class to the Duke of Edinburgh and the Queen Elizabeth carriers.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke proposes Duke of Edinburgh as the class name, partly because it creates a fitting escort relationship with HMS Queen Elizabeth. He also suggests Princess Royal as an individual ship name. The answer illustrates how Royal Navy naming traditions can reinforce institutional identity and public recognition.
segments.question: If the Royal Navy adopted the proposed Type 83 concept, what should the class be called?
segments.answerShort: Duke of Edinburgh class, with Princess Royal among the possible ship names.

evidence.note: The question and proposed class and ship names are stated directly.

segments.title: What is the largest warship Huascar could defeat?
segments.summary: The panel tests Huascar against contemporary ironclads while accounting for unreliable early torpedoes and slow gunnery.
segments.body: By guns and armor alone, Huascar could plausibly handle earlier central-battery and barbette ironclads and might exploit rate of fire against larger but slower designs. Her underwater Whitehead torpedoes create a theoretical chance against much larger ships, but their range, accuracy, and reliability were poor. The answer therefore separates possible kills from repeatable combat effectiveness.
segments.question: What is the largest technologically contemporary ship Huascar could take on and defeat?
segments.answerShort: Earlier ironclads are credible opponents; torpedoes give a remote chance against larger ships, but early Whitehead reliability makes that highly uncertain.

evidence.note: The panel identifies plausible ironclad opponents and the theoretical effect of torpedoes.
They qualify the answer with early Whitehead torpedo and gunnery reliability.

segments.title: A deliberately weak Manchurian battleship
segments.summary: A 1940 Japanese yard must build a prestigious-looking battleship for a Chinese client state without creating a threat to Japan.
segments.body: The challenge turns naval architecture into an instrument of political control. The ship must appear impressive, remain supportable at Port Arthur, and still be weak enough that Japanese cruisers could defeat it. The panel distinguishes between a design that is objectively poor and one that Japanese planners of the period would regard as safely inferior.

evidence.note: The client-state brief and its political and maintenance constraints are established.
The panel explains that Japan needs a fundamentally flawed ship it can readily overpower.

segments.title: USS Indiana as America's first battleship effort
segments.summary: USS Indiana is judged a strong first step for a navy rapidly moving beyond Civil War-era monitors.
segments.body: The ship had serious flaws, including unbalanced 13-inch guns that worsened list when trained outboard. Even so, the US Navy had moved from monitors through Maine and Texas to Indiana in roughly a decade. She compares poorly with mature foreign design systems, but rates well when judged as the beginning of a new American battleship tradition.
segments.question: How should USS Indiana BB-1 be rated as a first American battleship effort?
segments.answerShort: A good first effort despite important flaws, especially when measured against the US Navy's rapid transition from monitors rather than mature foreign designs.

evidence.note: The panel assesses Indiana's gun-balance flaw and her place in early US battleship development.

segments.title: Jet aircraft on straight-deck carriers
segments.summary: Straight flight decks could operate jets, while angled decks mainly improved landing safety and sortie flow.
segments.body: The angled flight deck did not make jet operation possible by itself. It addressed higher landing speeds and allowed launch and recovery cycles to be managed more safely and efficiently. Korean War operations demonstrate that straight-deck carriers could and did operate both jet and piston aircraft before angled decks became standard.
segments.question: Did the US Navy have difficulty operating jet aircraft from straight-deck carriers?
segments.answerShort: Not fundamentally; straight decks operated jets successfully, while angled decks solved landing-speed, safety, and sortie-rate problems.

evidence.note: The answer distinguishes jet operation from the operational benefits of angled decks and cites Korean War carriers.

segments.title: An early-1930s USS Constitution battlecruiser
segments.summary: The closing design asks what 30,000-33,000-ton US battlecruiser could merit the name Constitution in 1932.
segments.body: The designers must work near Second London Naval Treaty-era constraints while still producing a fast and prestigious capital ship. Hull choice limits gun caliber and forces decisions among 14- or 15-inch weapons, machinery, armor, and displacement. The exercise closes by comparing the completed American concepts and the symbolism expected of a ship named Constitution.

evidence.note: The final brief specifies a 30,000-33,000-ton early-1930s US battlecruiser.
Hull and gun-caliber limits shape the competing designs.
